results.title: ‎Shinobi: Read & Learn Japanese App - App Store
results.content: already building toward higher-level reading, Shinobi helps you turn reading into steady progress. Inside every story: • Tap any word for translations, readings, explanations, and examples • Full-page translation for quick comprehension • Furigana toggle to adjust the challenge level • Grammar explanations built directly into the reading experience Shinobi Japanese is not just about reading. It also helps you improve listening and speaking through audio and shadowing practice. Train your listening and speaking: • Native Japanese audio for every story • Slow audio mode for easier comprehension • Shadowing practice to improve pronunciation, rhythm, and speaking confidence Kanji and vocabulary are learned in context, not in isolation. This makes them easier to understand, recognize, and [...] I would pay to subscribe immediately (and I really want to!!), but there are two critical dealbreakers. First is the large font size isn’t nearly large enough. This is an accessibility issue and I’m sad the system text sizes don’t simply apply. All throughout the app there is tiny text that’s very hard to see for me, even with reading glasses on. I suspect there are some technical limitations here, but at the very least give us way more options. This alone makes the app super difficult for me. Second, real, native iPad support is woefully lacking and demonstrative of the understandably tiny team working on this app. Nothing scales well, leaving tons of empty space, for one, but most frustrating of all is that landscape mode and true window scaling isn’t supported. On iPadOS 26 in [...] Learn Japanese through illustrated stories, native audio, shadowing, quizzes, and interactive reading tools. Shinobi Japanese helps you build reading, kanji, vocabulary, grammar, listening, and speaking skills through content that feels immersive, practical, and actually enjoyable. Instead of memorizing isolated words and grammar points, you learn Japanese in context through stories, audio, news reading, and smart review tools. At the core of Shinobi Japanese are illustrated stories designed to make Japanese reading more accessible and more engaging. Each story unfolds page by page with Japanese text, visuals, and English support, helping you understand the language naturally while staying motivated. Whether you are a beginner or already building toward higher-level reading, Shinobi helps

results.title: Japanese Listening Practice: Methods That Actually Work
results.content:
How to shadow effectively:

A quick note on speed: Should you be listening to full speed native material for practice? Depends on your level. Beginners benefit more from slower, clearer speech. You can gradually increase speed as your comprehension improves. Jumping straight to full-speed native content when you're not ready just leads to frustration and burnout.

## Transcription practice: The hardcore method

Transcription means listening to audio and writing down exactly what you hear, word for word. This is hard. Like, really hard. But it's also one of the most effective ways to identify exactly what you're missing. [...] ## Why Japanese listening feels so hard at first

Japanese throws a bunch of challenges at you that English speakers aren't used to. The language has different pitch accents, particles that change meaning completely, and a ton of words that sound similar but mean totally different things. Plus, native speakers drop particles in casual conversation, slur words together, and use contractions that textbooks never taught you.

When you're a beginner, you might recognize individual words but lose track of where one word ends and another begins. That's completely normal. Your brain needs time to tune into the sound patterns of Japanese. [...] # Japanese Listening Practice: Methods of Listening Comprehension for Absolute Beginners and Advanced Learners

Last updated: December 19, 2025

japanese listening practice - Banner

Here's the thing about learning Japanese: you can memorize thousands of kanji, drill grammar patterns until your brain hurts, and still freeze up when someone actually speaks to you at normal speed. I've seen it happen to so many learners, and honestly, it's frustrating as hell. Your brain needs active engagement to build real listening comprehension. So let's talk about methods that actually improve your Japanese listening skills, starting from beginner level all the way up to understanding native speakers at full speed.

## Why Japanese listening feels so hard at first

It teaches you everything you need to know about listening, including how to train your brain to decode rapid speech (the 3rd week about ‘intensive listening’ is great for this). You'll build a rock-solid listening routine from the ground up, with smart and fun daily assignments that develop key listening habits, skills, and techniques—using Refold’s brilliant app to track your progress.

This course completely transformed my listening—turning it from a frustrating struggle into the most fun part of my language learning! The tools and strategies you’ll learn here will stay with you for months and years to come.

Includes content databases for 10 languages: Spanish, French, Italian, German, Japanese, Mandarin Chinese, Korean, Portuguese, Russian, and English. [...] Skip to Content 

Lingtuitive

# “I Don’t Understand Anything” - Why You Can’t Understand Fast Speech [and How to Fix it]

ListeningInput

Written By Christian Tapper

Frustrated young woman grabbing her hair while looking at a laptop screen, expressing difficulty and stress—perfectly capturing the struggle of understanding fast, native speech in a foreign language.

This article was last updated onJan 16, 2026.

### Table of Contents

 The Problem: Why it’s So Difficult to Understand Native Speakers

  + 1. People speak differently when speaking fast
  + 2. Listening is more difficult than reading
  + 3. There are too many unknown words

 The Solution: How to Fix it and Start Understanding Fast Speech [...] “How can I understand fast-talking native speakers?”

When we’re learning a new language we’re quickly faced with the problem that native, everyday speech is so difficult to follow. It feels like our brains and ears are being peppered by a machine gun. The words are thrown at us at a rate where it feels impossible to keep up. Understanding the words feels like trying to catch a fly with chopsticks (like Daniel-san in The Karate Kid).

Feels familiar? If so, you’re in good company. We all have to face this when learning a new language and it’s one of the most frustrating things in language learning! Native speakers speak at break-neck speed and make it sound so easy, but for us—it’s not.

results.title: Instagram
results.content:
Comment "waitlist" and I'll send you the link to try it.  
  
I accidentally went viral in China posting in a language I don't speak.  
  
My in-laws live there. They don't speak English. I don't speak Chinese. We communicate through smiles and broken Mandarin.  
  
So I started posting on Red Note — 350 million monthly active users, 90%+ don't speak English natively. My Chinese versions started outperforming my English originals. More views, more engagement, more clicks.  
  
I built createinchinese.com so any creator can do this. Upload a video, get Mandarin captions, Chinese thumbnails, Chinese titles. No translator needed.  
  
My mother-in-law follows my Red Note now and comments in English. That honestly means more than the numbers.  
  
Comment "waitlist" to get early access. [...] asaaab1001's profile picture
